title: Knowledge Management in Projects
summary: How to capture, share and reuse both tacit and explicit project knowledge so lessons are available at the moment of need.

sections.heading: Tacit vs explicit knowledge
sections.body:
Explicit knowledge is easily codified - documents, data, procedures and templates that can be written down and stored.

Tacit knowledge is personal know-how, experience, judgement and intuition that is hard to articulate. It lives in people's heads.

Managing project knowledge means sharing BOTH. Explicit knowledge moves through repositories and documents; tacit knowledge needs interaction - conversation, pairing, mentoring and observation - because it cannot be fully captured on a page.
sections.tip: If an answer can be copied into a document it is explicit; if it has to be demonstrated or discussed it is tacit.

sections.heading: The DIKW hierarchy
sections.body:
Knowledge is built up in stages: Data, then Information, then Knowledge, then Wisdom.

- Data: raw measurements and facts with no context
- Information: data placed in context so it has meaning
- Knowledge: information combined with experience to understand and act
- Wisdom: applying knowledge with sound judgement to make good decisions

The value rises at each level as raw numbers become understanding and, finally, good judgement.
sections.tip: Wisdom is knowing which knowledge to apply - and when not to.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 185' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><polygon points='150,18 36,172 264,172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.3'/><line x1='121' y1='58' x2='179' y2='58' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.7'/><line x1='92' y1='96' x2='208' y2='96' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.7'/><line x1='64' y1='134' x2='236' y2='134' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.7'/><text x='150' y='45' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Wisdom</text><text x='150' y='81' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Knowledge</text><text x='150' y='118' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Information</text><text x='150' y='156' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Data</text></svg>
figure.caption: DIKW: raw data becomes wisdom as context, experience, and judgement are added.

sections.heading: Manage Project Knowledge
sections.body:
This process draws on existing organisational knowledge - lessons learned from prior projects - AND creates new knowledge during the current project.

The aim is to make knowledge usable at the MOMENT of need, not to file it and forget it. Knowledge that is captured but never consulted adds no value.

Outputs feed the lessons learned register during the project and roll up into the lessons learned repository for the wider organisation at close.
sections.tip: Knowledge is an asset only when it reaches the right person at the right time.

sections.heading: Lessons learned vs retrospectives
sections.body:
Lessons learned are captured throughout the project and formally at close, primarily for the ORGANISATION's benefit on future work.

Agile retrospectives happen at the end of every iteration and focus on continuously improving the TEAM and its way of working right now.

The contrast is cadence and audience: lessons learned are periodic and outward-facing to the organisation; retrospectives are frequent and inward-facing to the team.
sections.tip: Same goal - learning - but retrospectives improve this team this sprint, lessons learned improve future projects.

sections.heading: Transferring knowledge
sections.body:
Use methods that move tacit knowledge, not just documents:

- Communities of practice that share a discipline
- Pairing and shadowing so know-how passes person to person
- Storytelling to convey context and reasoning
- Mentoring to grow judgement over time
- A usable, findable knowledge repository for explicit content

The usual barrier is behaviour - people simply do not consult what exists - so build knowledge checks into planning and review checkpoints.
sections.tip: Pull beats push: design the work so people reach for prior knowledge naturally.

sections.heading: Why knowledge is lost and how to keep it
sections.body:
Knowledge is lost when it is captured too late, stored where no one can find it, or never rewarded, and when staff leave taking tacit know-how with them.

To keep it: capture at the right moment while detail is fresh, make it findable and well organised, and recognise people who share.

A blameless culture is essential - if surfacing a problem invites punishment, honest lessons stay hidden and the same mistakes repeat.
sections.tip: No blame, no fear, no hidden lessons - psychological safety is the foundation of real learning.

source: PM Cram study notes — reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam.
